one_line_takeaway: A survey arguing it is the first to jointly map privacy-preserving and fairness research in federated learning, cataloging attacks, defenses (HE/SMC/DP), fairness notions, and the open challenge of reconciling privacy, fairness, and model performance simultaneously.
key_finding: As a survey, the paper's central takeaways are structural rather than numerical: (1) FL's privacy is not automatic — despite keeping raw data local, shared gradients/model updates leak information, exposing FL to inference attacks (inferring class representatives via GANs, membership inference, property inference) and poisoning attacks, typically by an honest-but-curious server or adversarial participants. (2) Standard defenses — Homomorphic Encryption, Secure Multiparty Computation, and Differential Privacy — each trade off against cost/accuracy (HE is computationally expensive; SMC has heavy communication overhead; DP degrades accuracy). (3) Fairness is under-served: the authors find only one prior comprehensive fairness-in-FL survey [161], and note it omitted group fairness; and no prior survey combined privacy and fairness — the identified gap this survey fills. (4) Open challenges include: lack of a metric for temporal/effort costs in contribution evaluation, missing/limited sensitive attributes, immature privacy-preservation in Vertical FL, privacy heterogeneity across devices, and the limitation of single-server (monopolistic) FL settings. The paper reports NO experimental quantitative results of its own.
core_contribution: A survey (Rafi, Noor, Hussain, Chae; arXiv:2306.08402v2, cs.CR) that the authors claim is the first to jointly review BOTH privacy-preserving AND fairness in federated learning (FL) in a single work — prior surveys treated these two concerns separately. Its own contribution is not a new method but a structured taxonomy and literature synthesis: (1) FL fundamentals and categorization (HFL/VFL/FTL) and the four FL phases; (2) privacy threats/adversaries in FL; (3) privacy-preservation techniques and privacy metrics; (4) fairness notions, sources of bias, and works on group/individual fairness; (5) the small set of works that address fairness and privacy jointly; and (6) open challenges and future research directions.
threat_model: The survey (grounded in Section 3) describes the FL threat landscape rather than proposing one: adversaries are a hostile/honest-but-curious central server that infers sensitive information from clients' model/gradient updates over time or meddles with training, and adversarial participants who can deduce other participants' confidential data, manipulate the parameter-aggregation process, or corrupt the global model. Privacy attacks occur mainly during the model-weight-exchange and inference phases and include inferring class representatives (via GAN-generated synthetic samples), membership inference (active and passive), and property inference; training-time attacks include model-poisoning and data-poisoning. Inference attacks assume attackers with unlimited compute, strong technical capability, and full model knowledge.
limitations: As a survey it contributes no new method, algorithm, or empirical evaluation, so its claims of taxonomic completeness and 'first combined' novelty are self-asserted and not experimentally validated. The five comparison tables are qualitative (advantages/disadvantages, existing-studies lists) and provide no reproducible benchmark numbers, making cross-method trade-offs (privacy vs. fairness vs. accuracy) descriptive rather than quantified. Coverage is a snapshot up to mid-2023 (v2, 14 Jul 2023). The authors themselves flag that fairness-aware AND privacy-preserving FL is served by only a very limited body of work, so the joint-treatment sections are necessarily thin.

datasets_used: None — this is a literature survey; the authors run no experiments and use no datasets of their own.
quantitative_results: The paper reports no quantitative experimental results of its own; it is a qualitative survey. Its five tables (Tables 1-5) summarize and qualitatively compare cited prior works (privacy techniques HE/SMC/DP, privacy-in-FL works, fairness notions, group-fairness works, and joint fairness+privacy works) rather than presenting measured metrics. The only 'counts' stated are bibliographic: the authors note just one prior comprehensive fairness-in-FL survey existed [161] and that no prior survey had combined privacy and fairness.
baselines_compared: None in an experimental sense (no method is proposed or benchmarked). The survey qualitatively contrasts prior FL surveys — e.g. a fairness-only survey [161], privacy/security threat surveys [209], [118], a privacy-issues survey [119], and a privacy-preserving-aggregation survey [113] — to position itself as the first combined privacy+fairness survey.
